solution.p: We will factor x^{2}+12xy+27y^{2} where a=1, b=12, and c=27.
Mental Method: When we factor a polynomial in the form of x^2+bx+c, x^2+bxy+cy^2, or x^2y^2+bxy+c we can use mental math to factor. We need to find two numbers whose product is c, and whose sum is b.

For x^{2}+12xy+27y^{2}, since the product of 9 and 3 is 27, and the sum of 9 and 3 is 12, the factorization is \begin{aligned}[t]x^{2}+12xy+27y^{2}\amp =\left(x+9y\right)\mathopen{}\left(x+3y\right)\end{aligned}
AC Method: In this method, we first calculate the product of a and c in ax^2+bx+c. For this problem, we have (1) \cdot (27) = 27.

The number 27 can be factored into the product of two numbers in the following ways:  egin{array}{lll}1(27)\amp -1(-27)\amp 3(9)\amp -3(-9)
ewline\end{array} Looking at the corresponding sums, we want a sum of 12, and this sum happens with 3+9=12 So we write  egin{aligned}[t]x^{2}+12xy+27y^{2}\amp =x^{2}+3xy+9xy+27y^{2}
ewline \amp =x\mathopen{}\left(x+3yight)+9y\mathopen{}\left(x+3yight)
ewline \amp =\left(x+9yight)\mathopen{}\left(x+3yight)\end{aligned}Generic Rectangles Method: First, we set up generic rectangles by putting the first term and third term in x^{2}+12xy+27y^{2} into the top left and bottom right rectangles:
The first term's coefficient, 1, can be factored into the product of two numbers (where the first factor is positive) in the following ways: \begin{array}{llll}1(1)\amp \end{array}
The third term's coefficient, 27, can be factored into the product of two numbers in the following ways: \begin{array}{llll}1(27)\amp -1(-27)\amp 3(9)\amp -3(-9)\newline\end{array}

We put each pair into the corresponding places next to those generic rectangles, and try to match the area of those rectangles with x^{2}+12xy+27y^{2} by guess-and-check. The following generic rectangles show the solution:
Dimensions of those generic rectangles in the diagram give us the solution: \begin{aligned}[t]x^{2}+12xy+27y^{2}\amp =\left(x+9y\right)\mathopen{}\left(x+3y\right)\end{aligned}
